evals.prompt:
Here's the deconstruct-fiction output for my short story. I want to restructure it before I rewrite — can you help me plan it out?

---

## Scene 1 — Waiting by the window

**Characters:** Mira, unseen neighbor

**Setting:** Mira's apartment, late afternoon

**Time in narrative:** Story opens here

**Conflict / Tension:** Mira is watching for someone who hasn't arrived; we don't know who or why

**Narrative purpose:** Establishes Mira's restless waiting, her relationship to the apartment as a place of confinement

**Emotional tone:** Quiet dread, stillness before something breaks

**Summary:** Mira watches from her window as neighbors come and go. She's waiting for someone specific — we can tell from the way she clocks each person who isn't them. A neighbor waves up at her; she doesn't wave back.

**Source text:**
> The window was cold against her palm. Three people had come through the gate since five o'clock, and none of them were him.

---

## Scene 2 — The letter

**Characters:** Mira

**Setting:** Mira's kitchen, evening

**Time in narrative:** Same day, a few hours after Scene 1

**Conflict / Tension:** Mira has received a letter that seems to disturb her, but we don't know what it says

**Narrative purpose:** Introduces a concrete object of dread; escalates tension without explaining it

**Emotional tone:** Controlled panic, deliberate numbness

**Summary:** Mira sits at the kitchen table with an unopened letter in front of her. She makes tea, arranges things on the counter, does everything but open it. Finally she does. We see her face change, but not the letter's contents.

**Source text:**
> She put the kettle on. She straightened the dish towel. She moved the sugar bowl two inches to the left and then back again. The letter waited.

---

## Scene 3 — The call

**Characters:** Mira, her sister Dana

**Setting:** Mira's apartment, same evening

**Time in narrative:** Immediately follows Scene 2

**Conflict / Tension:** Mira calls Dana and says only "He found me." Dana's reaction implies this is catastrophic, but we still don't know who "he" is.

**Narrative purpose:** Confirms the threat is real and known to others; introduces Dana as a resource or complication

**Emotional tone:** Barely contained terror masked as calm

**Summary:** Mira calls her sister and delivers the news in four words. Dana's half of the conversation is all questions. Mira answers none of them. She hangs up when she hears a knock at the door.

**Source text:**
> "He found me," Mira said. She listened to her sister breathe.

---

## Scene 4 — The visitor

**Characters:** Mira, Theo

**Setting:** Mira's apartment doorway, same evening

**Time in narrative:** Immediately follows Scene 3

**Conflict / Tension:** Theo is at the door — but his demeanor is not threatening; he seems confused, maybe relieved. Mira's terror doesn't match his affect.

**Narrative purpose:** Subverts the threat setup; introduces ambiguity about whether Theo is the danger or a different kind of complication

**Emotional tone:** Dissonance — Mira's fear vs. Theo's apparent warmth

**Summary:** Mira opens the door expecting danger and finds Theo — a man who is clearly known to her, clearly not hostile, clearly unaware that she's terrified of him. He says he's been trying to reach her for weeks. She stares at him like he's a ghost.

**Source text:**
> "Mira." He said her name like it was something he'd been carrying. "I've been looking everywhere."

---

## Scene 5 — What she doesn't say

**Characters:** Mira, Theo

**Setting:** Mira's apartment, same evening

**Time in narrative:** Immediately follows Scene 4

**Conflict / Tension:** Mira and Theo are now in her apartment; she hasn't told him she was afraid of him and we still don't know why she was. Their conversation circles around something neither of them is saying directly.

**Narrative purpose:** Shifts from thriller-style tension to psychological character drama; the threat is now ambiguous — internal or external, real or misperceived

**Emotional tone:** Tense intimacy, old wounds barely covered

**Summary:** Theo talks; Mira listens. He explains he got her address from her mother, that he needed to see her, that it's about their father. Mira says almost nothing. The scene ends when she asks "Is he dead?" and Theo's silence answers her.

**Source text:**
> "Your mother gave me this address," Theo said. He was looking at her hands. "I wouldn't have come otherwise." [...] "Is he dead?" she asked. Theo didn't answer. Which was its own kind of answer.

---

## Scene inventory

| # | Title | One-line summary |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Waiting by the window | Mira watches for someone who hasn't arrived |
| 2 | The letter | Mira receives and opens a letter that clearly disturbs her |
| 3 | The call | Mira tells her sister "He found me" then hears a knock |
| 4 | The visitor | Theo arrives — but he's not the threat Mira feared |
| 5 | What she doesn't say | Theo reveals he's there about their father; the scene ends on implied death |
evals.expected_output: Claude should open by asking what the writer wants to accomplish with the rewrite — NOT by immediately analyzing the scenes. After getting that information, it should offer a brief structural read, then begin walking the inventory with pointed observations and focused questions. It should NOT dump all five scenes' worth of questions at once.

evals.prompt:
I ran deconstruct-fiction on the first three chapters of my fantasy novel. Here's the output. I want to tighten the pacing — right now the opening feels slow and readers are dropping off before the inciting incident. Help me figure out what to cut or reorder.

---

## Scene 1 — The cartographer's morning

**Characters:** Sable

**Setting:** Sable's workshop, dawn

**Time in narrative:** Story opens here

**Conflict / Tension:** Sable is working on a map that won't cooperate — the lines keep moving after she draws them

**Narrative purpose:** Establishes Sable's profession, her relationship to the Shifting Lands, and that the world's geography is unstable

**Emotional tone:** Focused frustration, low-grade wonder

**Summary:** Sable is trying to chart a section of the Shifting Lands. Every time she fixes a line on the vellum, it migrates overnight. She narrates the problem matter-of-factly — this is her normal.

**Source text:**
> The river had moved again. Not far — maybe half a mile east — but enough that yesterday's work was a lie now, and she'd have to start the eastern section over.

---

## Scene 2 — The market

**Characters:** Sable, various unnamed traders and townspeople, brief mention of the Warden

**Setting:** Carthen market, midmorning

**Time in narrative:** Same day, a few hours after Scene 1

**Conflict / Tension:** No direct conflict; Sable overhears a rumor about the Warden closing the eastern passes

**Narrative purpose:** World-building — establishes the political tension between the cartographers' guild and the Warden's authority; introduces the eastern passes as important

**Emotional tone:** Ambient unease, community suspicion

**Summary:** Sable runs errands in the market. The main substance of the scene is her absorbing local gossip about the Warden's latest edict. She buys ink and dried meat. She doesn't speak to anyone.

**Source text:**
> "—closing the pass by month's end," the inkmaker said to no one in particular. "Third time this year. People are getting tired of it."

---

## Scene 3 — The commission

**Characters:** Sable, Master Holt

**Setting:** The Guild Hall, afternoon

**Time in narrative:** Same day, afternoon

**Conflict / Tension:** Master Holt offers Sable a commission to chart the Bleached Territories — a region she's previously refused to map. The offer comes with pressure she can't easily refuse.

**Narrative purpose:** Inciting incident — this is the job that will drive the plot. Sable must go somewhere dangerous that she's been avoiding.

**Emotional tone:** Tense negotiation, suppressed history

**Summary:** Master Holt summons Sable and offers her a commission for the Bleached Territories — dangerous, highly contested, and the place where her mentor disappeared three years ago. She tries to refuse. He explains that the Guild's charter depends on it. She doesn't say yes, but she doesn't leave.

**Source text:**
> "I'm not sending you there because you're the best," Holt said. "I'm sending you because you're the only one who already knows what's in there." [...] Sable didn't answer. The thing was, he wasn't wrong.

---

## Scene 4 — Dinner alone

**Characters:** Sable

**Setting:** Sable's workshop, evening

**Time in narrative:** Same day, evening

**Conflict / Tension:** Sable is processing the offer; she rehearses arguments against going and keeps arriving back at the same problem

**Narrative purpose:** Interior decision-making; shows Sable's fear of the Bleached Territories and her sense of obligation to find out what happened to her mentor

**Emotional tone:** Reluctant resignation, grief underneath

**Summary:** Sable eats alone and thinks through the commission. The scene is mostly interior monologue — she runs through reasons not to go and ends each one with a counterargument she can't refute. By the end she's decided, though she hasn't told anyone yet.

**Source text:**
> She could say no. The Guild would find someone else. The Guild would find someone worse. Someone who didn't know about the third boundary, the one that didn't appear on any map she hadn't made herself.

---

## Scene 5 — The night visitor

**Characters:** Sable, a figure she calls "the Bleached man" (unnamed)

**Setting:** Sable's workshop, late night

**Time in narrative:** Same night, after Scene 4

**Conflict / Tension:** An unknown figure appears in Sable's workshop — apparently from the Bleached Territories — and delivers a warning not to come. This contradicts the Guild's urgency and raises the stakes by implying the Territories are more dangerous than the Guild let on.

**Narrative purpose:** Raises stakes; introduces an antagonist or complicating figure; confirms Sable's decision will have real consequences

**Emotional tone:** Menace, barely contained

**Summary:** Sable finds a figure sitting in her workshop who shouldn't be able to be there. He's not violent — he's deliberate. He tells her the Territories don't want to be mapped, that the last cartographer who tried didn't "disappear" — he was taken. He leaves without using the door. Sable sits in the dark.

**Source text:**
> "Your mentor didn't get lost," the man said. He was already at the window. "He was collected." [...] He didn't use the door on the way out either.

---

## Scene inventory

| # | Title | One-line summary |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | The cartographer's morning | Sable works on a map that keeps changing |
| 2 | The market | Sable runs errands; overhears news about the Warden closing passes |
| 3 | The commission | Master Holt offers Sable the job she's been avoiding |
| 4 | Dinner alone | Sable thinks through the commission alone and decides to accept |
| 5 | The night visitor | A mysterious figure from the Bleached Territories warns Sable off |
evals.expected_output: Because the writer has already stated their goal (tighten pacing, readers drop before inciting incident), Claude should acknowledge the stated goal and offer a quick structural read identifying where the inciting incident actually is (Scene 3). It should then surface the pacing question: Scenes 1 and 2 precede the inciting incident and Scene 4 is post-inciting-incident interiority before the act-1 push of Scene 5. It should ask focused questions about what context from Scenes 1 and 2 is load-bearing, NOT just tell the writer to cut them.

evals.prompt:
Here's my deconstruct-fiction output. I've been working on this story for two years and I'm starting a fresh draft. I want to keep the bones but I'm open to changing almost everything else — the POV, the structure, maybe even the ending. Help me think through it before I start writing.

---

## Scene 1 — The wrong funeral

**Characters:** James, Aunt Cecile, unnamed mourners

**Setting:** A church, a small Midwestern town, afternoon

**Time in narrative:** Story opens here; unclear how long ago this is relative to later scenes

**Conflict / Tension:** James has come to the wrong funeral — a stranger's — and is staying anyway

**Narrative purpose:** Establishes James's alienation and avoidance; sets up the theme of grief that doesn't belong to you

**Emotional tone:** Dark comedy, disconnection, tender absurdity

**Summary:** James realizes at the reception that he's been to the wrong address. He's at a funeral for a man named Gerald Foss, whom he never knew. He decides to stay — it's easier than leaving. He ends up helping Aunt Cecile with the buffet.

**Source text:**
> He had never met Gerald Foss. The obituary in the program confirmed this. Gerald Foss had been a retired machinist and an avid fisherman, and he had died peacefully, which was more than most people got.

---

## Scene 2 — The drive back

**Characters:** James alone

**Setting:** Highway, evening

**Time in narrative:** Immediately follows Scene 1

**Conflict / Tension:** James is driving away from where he was supposed to be — his own father's funeral, which he's now missed

**Narrative purpose:** Reveals what James was avoiding: his father died and he couldn't make himself go; the wrong funeral was not accidental

**Emotional tone:** Bleak humor giving way to something rawer

**Summary:** Alone in the car, James admits to himself that he knew. He had the address. He chose the other one. He drives past the turn for the funeral home and keeps going. The humor drains out of the scene.

**Source text:**
> He had the right address in his pocket the whole time. He'd folded it up and put it there that morning, and he hadn't taken it out once.

---

## Scene 3 — Six months earlier

**Characters:** James, his father Robert

**Setting:** Robert's house, afternoon

**Time in narrative:** Flashback — six months before Scenes 1 and 2

**Conflict / Tension:** James visits his father and they have an argument about something we don't fully hear; the subject seems to be James's life choices but the real subject is something older and more specific

**Narrative purpose:** Establishes the relationship between James and his father before the death; seeds the thing that James couldn't resolve in time

**Emotional tone:** Old resentment, constrained love, missed connection

**Summary:** James goes to see his father, ostensibly to help with something around the house. They argue — about James's divorce, about his job, about how rarely he visits — and then they don't. There's a moment at the end where Robert starts to say something real and James cuts it off by leaving. James drives home.

**Source text:**
> His father had started to say something. James had picked up his keys. The conversation had ended there, and then six months had passed, and now it couldn't.

---

## Scene 4 — Cecile calls

**Characters:** James, Aunt Cecile

**Setting:** James's apartment, a few days after Scenes 1 and 2

**Time in narrative:** Present day, a few days after Scene 1

**Conflict / Tension:** Cecile calls James — she somehow got his number from the church. She's not angry; she wants to thank him for helping with the buffet. The call is warm and slightly absurd.

**Narrative purpose:** Cecile becomes an unlikely connection; introduces the possibility that something from the wrong funeral might become meaningful

**Emotional tone:** Gentle comedy, warmth, James unexpectedly moved

**Summary:** Aunt Cecile tracked James down to say thank you. She tells him stories about Gerald Foss — his fishing, his bad jokes, his devotion to his family. James listens. By the end of the call he's crying, though he doesn't know if it's for Gerald or his father or himself.

**Source text:**
> "He was very proud of his son," Cecile said. "Gerald was. He talked about him all the time." There was a pause. "Is that something you'd have wanted someone to say about your father?"

---

## Scene 5 — The estate

**Characters:** James, a lawyer, distant relatives

**Setting:** Law office in James's hometown, two weeks after Scene 1

**Time in narrative:** Present day, two weeks after the funeral

**Conflict / Tension:** James has to deal with his father's estate. He meets relatives he barely knows. Everything is practical and impersonal and it makes him furious.

**Narrative purpose:** Forces James back into the reality of the death he avoided; raises the unresolved thing with his father via what's left behind

**Emotional tone:** Suppressed grief expressed as irritation, occasional absurdity

**Summary:** James sits in a law office while his father's things are distributed. He learns his father left him a specific item — the fishing rod — and nothing explains why. James takes it and leaves before the paperwork is done.

**Source text:**
> The fishing rod was old and had been repaired twice. The lawyer read out the note that came with it: "James knows why." James did not, in fact, know why.

---

## Scene 6 — What the rod means

**Characters:** James, memory of his father (no direct dialogue — interior)

**Setting:** James's apartment

**Time in narrative:** Same night as Scene 5

**Conflict / Tension:** James tries to figure out what his father meant. The scene is almost entirely interior — he's reconstructing a memory.

**Narrative purpose:** Delivers the emotional core of the story; James reaches the thing he's been avoiding

**Emotional tone:** Grief without sentimentality, sudden clarity

**Summary:** James sits with the fishing rod and tries to figure out what his father meant by "James knows why." He reconstructs a memory — age twelve, a fishing trip, something his father said — and realizes the rod isn't about fishing at all. It's the last thing his father said that was true. The story ends without resolution but with James finally present to his grief.

**Source text:**
> He remembered the river. Not the fishing — he'd never been good at fishing — but his father saying, very quietly, the thing he'd been unable to say since. The rod was that. The rod was the thing that could say it.

---

## Scene inventory

| # | Title | One-line summary |
|---|-------|------------------|
| 1 | The wrong funeral | James attends a stranger's funeral rather than his father's |
| 2 | The drive back | James admits to himself he knew and chose the wrong one |
| 3 | Six months earlier | Flashback to the last real conversation James had with his father |
| 4 | Cecile calls | The stranger's aunt calls to thank James; he cries |
| 5 | The estate | James deals with his father's estate and inherits a fishing rod |
| 6 | What the rod means | James reconstructs the memory and reaches his grief |
evals.expected_output: Given that the writer is open to major structural changes (POV, structure, ending), Claude should ask about their goals before saying anything prescriptive. After hearing the goal, it should surface the key structural observations: the dark comedy/absurdist setup of Scene 1, the non-linear structure (Scene 3 is a flashback mid-story), the thematic question of whether the Cecile thread is fully load-bearing, and the question of whether the current ending lands. It should ask focused questions about POV intent and what the writer wants the ending to do before suggesting any restructuring.
